summary:
The Crimean Khanate (1441–1783 AD) was a Tatar Muslim state on the Crimean Peninsula and adjacent steppe that served as a major power in Eastern European politics for over three centuries — a vassalate of the [[event:2400|Ottoman Empire]] that raided deep into Poland-Lithuania and Russia, supplying enslaved Slavic captives to the Ottoman and Mediterranean slave markets in enormous numbers. The khanate was founded by Haci I Giray, a descendant of Genghis Khan, after the fragmentation of the Golden Horde; its cavalry raiders ranged as far north as Moscow (which they burned in 1571) and Lublin, capturing an estimated 2 million slaves between the 16th and 17th centuries — a massive economic and demographic drain on the Ukrainian and Russian populations.

The Crimean Khanate's decline was gradual, driven by the rising power of Russia. The successive [[event:2445|Russo-Turkish Wars]] of the 18th century progressively weakened Ottoman ability to support the khanate, and Catherine the Great's forces annexed Crimea directly in 1783 — a peaceful annexation (the last Crimean Khan abdicated without resistance) that added a warm-water Black Sea coastline to the Russian Empire. The annexation of Crimea was one of Catherine's most significant strategic achievements, giving Russia its first warm-water naval base (Sevastopol, founded 1783) and dramatically extending Russian power toward the Mediterranean. The name 'Crimea' would recur in Russian geopolitical ambition into the modern era: the Crimean War (1853–1856) and Russia's annexation in 2014 both continued this centuries-old strategic logic.
